On 1 December 1991, benefits for out-of-hospital GP attendances for non-card holders were reduced by $3.50. GPs could charge a copayment for bulk billed services of up to $2.50 to compensate for the benefit reduction. In addition, GPs were paid a $1 transaction fee to cover the cost of collecting the copayment. Examples of card holders were persons covered by a Health Care Card, Pensioner Health Benefits Card and Specific Treatment Entitlement Card. Copayments for bulk billing and the benefit reduction ceased from 1 March 1992.

On 1 December 1991, benefits for out-of-hospital GP attendances for non-card holders were reduced by $3.50. GPs could charge a copayment for bulk billed services of up to $2.50 to compensate for the benefit reduction. In addition, GPs were paid a $1 transaction fee to cover the cost of collecting the copayment. Examples of card holders were persons covered by a Health Care Card, Pensioner Health Benefits Card and Specific Treatment Entitlement Card. Copayments for bulk billing and the benefit reduction ceased from 1 March 1992.

On 1 December 1991, benefits for out-of-hospital GP attendances for non-card holders were reduced by $3.50. GPs could charge a copayment for bulk billed services of up to $2.50 to compensate for the benefit reduction. In addition, GPs were paid a $1 transaction fee to cover the cost of collecting the copayment. Examples of card holders were persons covered by a Health Care Card, Pensioner Health Benefits Card and Specific Treatment Entitlement Card. Copayments for bulk billing and the benefit reduction ceased from 1 March 1992.

On 1 February 2004, the $4 billion Strengthening Medicare package in response to falling rates of bulk billing was introduced. Bulk billing incentives introduced for non-referred services involving children under 16 and concessional patients.

On 1 May 2004, the introduction of additional bulk billing incentive item, 10991 to provide a higher incentive payment (compared to item 10990) for the bulk billing of children under 16 and concessional patients for attendances provided in regional, rural or remote areas, areas of health workforce shortage or the whole of Tasmania. Eligible areas have later been defined by Modified Monash areas.

On 1 January 2005, rebates for non-hospital GP attendances increased to 100% of Schedule fee (not appliable to bulk billing incentive items). Also introduced was item 10992, which provided another incentive for the bulk billing of children under 16 and concessional patients receiving after-hours attendances provided in the areas defined under item 10991, in the specific case of the GP not being based in the eligible area, and therefore unable to claim item 10991.
After 1 July 2014, a freeze on indexation to Schedule fees was introduced (known as the Medicare rebate freeze).
From 1 July 2017, re-introduction of indexation of Schedule fees in a phased lifting approach commenced. However, indexation did not extend to all Medicare items.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

From 13 March 2020, new telehealth items were introduced to protect patients and doctors during the COVID-19 pandemic. There was a temporary doubling of Schedule fees for all bulk billing incentive items between 30 March and 30 September. This included 2 temporary GP and other medical practitioner bulk billing incentive items for patients who are vulnerable to COVID-19, introduced on 20 April and ended on 30 September.

On 26 February 2021 GP attendance items were introduced for assessing a patient's suitability for a COVID-19 vaccine. It is a requirement that these items are bulk billed. In addition, bulk billing incentives are not payable for these items.

On 1 November 2023, new bulk billing items with higher incentive payments were introduced for certain types of GP consultations outside of hospital, for children under 16 and concessional patients. These payments are triple the amount of existing standard bulk billing incentive payments. In addition, the standard bulk billing incentive payments continue to be available for the remaining types of GP consultations.

On 1 July 2025, COVID-19 vaccine support items were removed from the MBS. These were items claimed by GPs to assess a patient's suitability for the COVID-19 vaccine, and were removed to bring the MBS treatment of COVID-19 vaccines in line with other vaccines. GPs will be able to use general attendance items when providing COVID-19 vaccine support services.

On 1 November 2025, bulk billing incentives, previously only eligible for patients under 16 years of age or who were a Commonwealth concession cardholder, were expanded to all Medicare-eligible patients when bulk billed.
